item.title: - What fetters the world? What leads the world? By abandoning what thing Are all fetters cut? - Delight fetters the world, Seeking leads the…
item.description:
- What fetters the world?
What leads the world?
By abandoning what thing
Are all fetters cut?
- Delight fetters the world,
Seeking leads the world,
By abandoning craving,
All fetters are cut.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 1.65
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, there is no form that is permanent, everlasting, eternal, not subject to change, that will remain forever. There is no feeling … perception ……
item.description:
“Monks, there is no form that is permanent, everlasting, eternal, not subject to change, that will remain forever. There is no feeling … perception … formation … consciousness that is permanent, everlasting, eternal, not subject to change, that will remain forever.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.98
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Having seen with wisdom, the defilements are abandoned. Monks, that monk is called one who has blinded Māra, destroyed his track, escaped the sight of…
item.description:
“Having seen with wisdom, the defilements are abandoned. Monks, that monk is called one who has blinded Māra, destroyed his track, escaped the sight of Māra. He has crossed beyond all clinging in the world. He walks confidently, stands confidently, sits confidently, lies down confidently. Why is that? Because he has escaped the reach of Māra.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 26 – Ariyapariyesanāsutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Furthermore, monks, a noble disciple reflects: ‘Sensual pleasures present and future, perceptions of sensual pleasure present and future, forms present and future, perceptions of forms…
item.description:
“Furthermore, monks, a noble disciple reflects: ‘Sensual pleasures present and future, perceptions of sensual pleasure present and future, forms present and future, perceptions of forms present and future — both are impermanent. What is impermanent is not fit to delight in, not fit to welcome, not fit to cling to.’ While he practices thus, dwells often thus, his mind becomes serene in that element. With serene mind, he attains the Imperturbable either in this very life or he becomes strongly inclined to wisdom. At the breakup of the body, after death, it is possible that his consciousness, proceeding accordingly, may attain the Imperturbable.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 106 – Āneñjasappāyasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Behold this decorated image — A heap of wounds, a collection of sores, Diseased, the object of many lustful thoughts, Nothing stable or permanent here.…
item.description:
Behold this decorated image —
A heap of wounds, a collection of sores,
Diseased, the object of many lustful thoughts,
Nothing stable or permanent here.

Behold this adorned appearance,
With jewels and earrings,
A skeleton covered by skin,
Made beautiful by clothes.

The feet are painted with lac and dye,
The face is smeared with powder and paste —
Enough to fool a fool,
But not one who seeks the far shore.

The hair is curled in seven rings,
The eyes are rubbed with collyrium and rouge —
Enough to fool a fool,
But not one who seeks the far shore.

From: Majjhima Nikāya 82 – Raṭṭhapālasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, I will teach you about suffering, the origin of suffering, the cessation of suffering, and the path leading to the cessation of suffering. Listen.…
item.description:
“Monks, I will teach you about suffering, the origin of suffering, the cessation of suffering, and the path leading to the cessation of suffering. Listen. And what, monks, is suffering? It should be said: the five aggregates of clinging. What five? The form aggregate of clinging … the consciousness aggregate of clinging. This, monks, is called suffering. And what, monks, is the origin of suffering? It is this craving that leads to renewed existence … craving for non-existence. This, monks, is called the origin of suffering. And what, monks, is the cessation of suffering? It is the remainderless fading away and cessation of that same craving, the abandoning and relinquishing of it, freedom from it, non-reliance. This, monks, is called the cessation of suffering. And what, monks, is the path leading to the cessation of suffering? It is the Noble Eightfold Path, namely: right view … right concentration. This, monks, is called the path leading to the cessation of suffering.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.104
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Thoughts of lust Are the sensual pleasures of humans, The beautiful things in the world Are not themselves sensual pleasures. Thoughts of lust Are the…
item.description:
Thoughts of lust
Are the sensual pleasures of humans,
The beautiful things in the world
Are not themselves sensual pleasures.
Thoughts of lust
Are the sensual pleasures of humans.
The beautiful things remain
As they are in the world,
But the wise here
Subdue their lust.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 6.63
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Craving, monks, is a net that widens the wandering, it sticks; because of this craving, this world is destroyed, bound, tangled like a ball of…
item.description:
“Craving, monks, is a net that widens the wandering, it sticks; because of this craving, this world is destroyed, bound, tangled like a ball of thread, like a tangled skein, like muñja grass and babaja reeds, unable to pass beyond the plane of misery, the bad destination, the lower realm, saṃsāra.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 4.199
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, just as a skilled carpenter or his apprentice might use a small peg to knock out, drive out, and remove a larger peg; so…
item.description:
“Monks, just as a skilled carpenter or his apprentice might use a small peg to knock out, drive out, and remove a larger peg; so too, monks, when a monk attends to some sign and gives attention to it, if evil, unwholesome thoughts connected with desire, ill-will, and delusion arise, then that monk should attend to a different sign connected with what is wholesome, not that sign. By attending to a different sign connected with what is wholesome, those evil, unwholesome thoughts connected with desire, ill-will, and delusion are abandoned and subside. With their abandonment, the mind becomes steadied, settled, unified, and concentrated.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 20 – Vitakkasaṇṭhānasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, if that monk, while attending to a different wholesome sign, still has evil, unwholesome thoughts connected with desire, ill-will, and delusion arising, then that…
item.description:
“Monks, if that monk, while attending to a different wholesome sign, still has evil, unwholesome thoughts connected with desire, ill-will, and delusion arising, then that monk should examine the peril in those thoughts: ‘These thoughts are unwholesome, these thoughts are blameworthy, these thoughts lead to painful results.’ By examining the peril in those thoughts, those evil, unwholesome thoughts are abandoned and subside. With their abandonment, the mind becomes steadied, settled, unified, and concentrated.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 20 – Vitakkasaṇṭhānasutta
📖 Read this sutta
